---
title: "Boltz Pulldown Analysis Report"
author: "Australian-Protein-Design-Initiative/nf-binder-design"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
jupyter: python3
---

# Overview

This report presents an analysis of the Boltz pulldown results, including statistical comparisons between targets, visualizations of binding scores, and enrichment analyses.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats
from scipy.stats import wilcoxon
from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests
import plotly.express as px
import altair as alt
import os
import re
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Set plotting style
plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8')

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('boltz_pulldown.tsv', sep='\t')

df = df.sort_values('iptm', ascending=False)

# Calculate ranks
df['rank_within_binder_normalized'] = df.groupby('binder')['iptm'].rank(ascending=False, pct=True)
df['inv_norm_rank'] = 1 - df['rank_within_binder_normalized']

# Helper function to convert RGB to Hex
def rgb_to_hex(rgb):
    """Converts an (R, G, B) tuple to a hex color string."""
    return f'#{int(rgb[0]*255):02x}{int(rgb[1]*255):02x}{int(rgb[2]*255):02x}'

# Create a consistent color palette for targets
targets_unique = df['target'].unique()
palette_rgb = sns.color_palette("husl", len(targets_unique))
# RGB map for seaborn/matplotlib
color_map_rgb = dict(zip(targets_unique, palette_rgb))
# Hex map for plotly
color_map_hex = {target: rgb_to_hex(color) for target, color in color_map_rgb.items()}

# Styling function to color the target cells
def color_target_cell(val):
    color = color_map_hex.get(val, '#ffffff') # Default to white
    # Also setting text to black for better contrast/readability
    return f'background-color: {color}; color: #000000'

print(f"Unique targets: {df['target'].nunique()}")
print(f"Unique binders: {df['binder'].nunique()}")
print(f"Score range (iptm): {df['iptm'].min():.3f} - {df['iptm'].max():.3f}")
```

# Analyses and visualizations

## Binder monomer re-prediction

> TODO: Are we showing 'pruned' or 'unpruned' RMSD here - we should output both at the RMSD4ALL stage. 
> Note for 'pruned' values because each pair of binders is pairwise aligned and are likely not sequence similar, the RMSD may be calculated over only a small sequence-similar region in many cases. This will have also have an impact on the diagonal in the table below, since even identical sequences will have non-superimposing parts pruned.

The structure of the binder alone ("monomer") was re-predicted from it's sequence only, and compared to it's original bound structure from the complex.

```{python}
# | label: binder-monomer-reprediction
# | message: false

# Check if the RMSD file exists
rmsd_file = "predicted-binder-monomers_vs_input-binders.tsv"

if os.path.exists(rmsd_file):
    # Read the RMSD matrix
    rmsd_df = pd.read_csv(rmsd_file, sep="\t", index_col=0)

    print(f"RMSD matrix dimensions: {rmsd_df.shape}")
    print(f"Matrix contains {rmsd_df.shape[0]} rows and {rmsd_df.shape[1]} columns")

    # Extract diagonal values (assuming square matrix)
    is_square = rmsd_df.shape[0] == rmsd_df.shape[1]
    if is_square:
        diagonal_values = []
        for i, col_name in enumerate(rmsd_df.columns):
            if i < len(rmsd_df.index):
                diagonal_value = rmsd_df.iloc[i, i]
                diagonal_values.append(
                    {"Column": col_name, "Diagonal_RMSD": diagonal_value}
                )

        # Create and display diagonal table
        diagonal_df = pd.DataFrame(diagonal_values)
        styled_diagonal = (
            diagonal_df.style.set_caption(
                "C-alpha RMSD Values (Predicted vs Input Complex)"
            )
            .format({"C-alpha RMSD": "{:.3f}"})
            .background_gradient(cmap="viridis", subset=["Diagonal_RMSD"])
            .hide(axis="index")
        )
        display(styled_diagonal)
    else:
        print(
            "Warning: Binder RMSD matrix is not square. Cannot extract diagonal values."
        )
        print("Displaying matrix as-is:")
        display(rmsd_df)

```

Hierarchically clustered heatmap of the C-alpha RMSD values between the every predicted binder monomer and every binders in the the input complex structures.

> TODO: Use TM-score instead of C-alpha RMSD for all-against-all binder comparisons ? - but something faster as TM-score is too slow for this many comparisons.

```{python}
# | label: binder-monomer-reprediction-heatmap
# | message: false

if os.path.exists(rmsd_file) and is_square:
    # Create hierarchically clustered heatmap
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))

    # Convert to numpy array for clustering
    rmsd_matrix = rmsd_df.values

    # Check for NaN values and handle them
    nan_count = np.isnan(rmsd_matrix).sum()
    if nan_count > 0:
        print(f"Warning: Found {nan_count} NaN values in the RMSD matrix.")
        print(
            "NaN values will be replaced with the maximum RMSD value for clustering purposes."
        )

        # Replace NaN values with the maximum finite value for clustering
        max_finite_value = np.nanmax(rmsd_matrix)
        rmsd_matrix_clean = np.where(
            np.isnan(rmsd_matrix), max_finite_value, rmsd_matrix
        )
        rmsd_df_clean = pd.DataFrame(
            rmsd_matrix_clean, index=rmsd_df.index, columns=rmsd_df.columns
        )
    else:
        rmsd_df_clean = rmsd_df

    # Create clustermap with hierarchical clustering
    g = sns.clustermap(
        rmsd_df_clean,
        cmap="viridis_r",  # Reverse colormap so lower RMSD = darker
        method="average",
        metric="euclidean",
        figsize=(12, 10),
        cbar_kws={"label": "RMSD (Å)"},
        xticklabels=False,
        yticklabels=False,
        annot=False,
        row_cluster=True,
        col_cluster=True,
    )  # Set to True if you want values displayed

    g.fig.suptitle(
        "RMSD Matrix: Predicted Binder Monomers vs Input Complex\n(Hierarchically Clustered)",
        y=1.02,
        fontsize=14,
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    # Print summary statistics (excluding NaN values)
    finite_values = rmsd_matrix[~np.isnan(rmsd_matrix)]
    print(f"\nRMSD Summary Statistics (excluding NaN values):")
    print(f"Mean RMSD: {finite_values.mean():.3f} Å")
    print(f"Median RMSD: {np.median(finite_values):.3f} Å")
    print(f"Min RMSD: {finite_values.min():.3f} Å")
    print(f"Max RMSD: {finite_values.max():.3f} Å")
    print(f"Std RMSD: {finite_values.std():.3f} Å")
    print(f"Total finite values: {len(finite_values)}")
    print(f"Total NaN values: {nan_count}")

    # else:
    #     print("Warning: RMSD matrix is not square. Cannot extract diagonal values.")
    #     print("Displaying matrix as-is:")
    #     display(rmsd_df)

else:
    print(f"RMSD file '{rmsd_file}' not found.")
    print(
        "This analysis requires the RMSD comparison between predicted binder monomers and input complex structures."
    )
    print(
        "The file should be generated by the RMSD4ALL process in the Nextflow pipeline."
    )
```

## TODO: Target monomer re-prediction - current pipeline results are wrong ?

The structure of the target alone ("monomer") was re-predicted from it's sequence only, and compared to it's original apo structure.

```{python}
# | label: target-monomer-reprediction
# | message: false

# Check if the target RMSD file exists
target_rmsd_file = "predicted-target-monomers_vs_input-targets.tsv"

if os.path.exists(target_rmsd_file):
    # Read the RMSD matrix
    target_rmsd_df = pd.read_csv(target_rmsd_file, sep="\t", index_col=0)

    print(f"Target RMSD matrix dimensions: {target_rmsd_df.shape}")
    print(
        f"Matrix contains {target_rmsd_df.shape[0]} rows and {target_rmsd_df.shape[1]} columns"
    )

    # Extract diagonal values (assuming square matrix)
    if target_rmsd_df.shape[0] == target_rmsd_df.shape[1]:
        target_diagonal_values = []
        for i, col_name in enumerate(target_rmsd_df.columns):
            if i < len(target_rmsd_df.index):
                diagonal_value = target_rmsd_df.iloc[i, i]
                target_diagonal_values.append(
                    {"Column": col_name, "Diagonal_RMSD": diagonal_value}
                )

        # Create and display diagonal table
        target_diagonal_df = pd.DataFrame(target_diagonal_values)
        styled_target_diagonal = (
            target_diagonal_df.style.set_caption(
                "Diagonal RMSD Values (Predicted Target Monomers vs Input Target)"
            )
            .format({"Diagonal_RMSD": "{:.3f}"})
            .background_gradient(cmap="viridis", subset=["Diagonal_RMSD"])
            .hide(axis="index")
        )
        display(styled_target_diagonal)

        # Create hierarchically clustered heatmap
        plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))

        # Convert to numpy array for clustering
        target_rmsd_matrix = target_rmsd_df.values

        # Check for NaN values and handle them
        target_nan_count = np.isnan(target_rmsd_matrix).sum()
        if target_nan_count > 0:
            print(
                f"Warning: Found {target_nan_count} NaN values in the target RMSD matrix."
            )
            print(
                "NaN values will be replaced with the maximum RMSD value for clustering purposes."
            )

            # Replace NaN values with the maximum finite value for clustering
            target_max_finite_value = np.nanmax(target_rmsd_matrix)
            target_rmsd_matrix_clean = np.where(
                np.isnan(target_rmsd_matrix),
                target_max_finite_value,
                target_rmsd_matrix,
            )
            target_rmsd_df_clean = pd.DataFrame(
                target_rmsd_matrix_clean,
                index=target_rmsd_df.index,
                columns=target_rmsd_df.columns,
            )
        else:
            target_rmsd_df_clean = target_rmsd_df

        # Create clustermap with hierarchical clustering
        g = sns.clustermap(
            target_rmsd_df_clean,
            cmap="viridis_r",  # Reverse colormap so lower RMSD = darker
            method="average",
            metric="euclidean",
            figsize=(12, 10),
            cbar_kws={"label": "RMSD (Å)"},
            xticklabels=False,
            yticklabels=False,
            annot=False,
            row_cluster=True,
            col_cluster=True,
        )

        g.fig.suptitle(
            "RMSD Matrix: Predicted Target Monomers vs Input Target\n(Hierarchically Clustered)",
            y=1.02,
            fontsize=14,
        )

        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

        # Print summary statistics (excluding NaN values)
        target_finite_values = target_rmsd_matrix[~np.isnan(target_rmsd_matrix)]
        print(f"\nTarget RMSD Summary Statistics (excluding NaN values):")
        print(f"Mean RMSD: {target_finite_values.mean():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Median RMSD: {np.median(target_finite_values):.3f} Å")
        print(f"Min RMSD: {target_finite_values.min():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Max RMSD: {target_finite_values.max():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Std RMSD: {target_finite_values.std():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Total finite values: {len(target_finite_values)}")
        print(f"Total NaN values: {target_nan_count}")

    else:
        print(
            "Warning: Target RMSD matrix is not square. Cannot extract diagonal values."
        )
        print("Displaying matrix as-is:")
        display(target_rmsd_df)

else:
    print(f"Target RMSD file '{target_rmsd_file}' not found.")
    print(
        "This analysis requires the RMSD comparison between predicted target monomers and input target structures."
    )
    print(
        "The file should be generated by the RMSD4ALL process in the Nextflow pipeline."
    )
```

## Complex re-prediction

The structure of the complex was re-predicted, and superimposed on the original and the C-alpha RMSD calculated (using binder sequence only, and the target with MSA and template structure provided).

```{python}
# | label: complex-reprediction
# | message: false

# Check if the complex RMSD file exists
complex_rmsd_file = "predicted-complexes_vs_input-complexes.tsv"

if os.path.exists(complex_rmsd_file):
    # Read the RMSD matrix
    complex_rmsd_df = pd.read_csv(complex_rmsd_file, sep="\t", index_col=0)

    print(f"Complex RMSD matrix dimensions: {complex_rmsd_df.shape}")
    print(
        f"Matrix contains {complex_rmsd_df.shape[0]} rows and {complex_rmsd_df.shape[1]} columns"
    )

    # Extract matching values based on column/row name patterns
    complex_matching_values = []
    col_design_ids = set()
    row_design_ids = set()

    for col_name in complex_rmsd_df.columns:
        # Extract the design identifier from column name (before .pdb)
        # binder_design_id.pdb -> binder_design_id
        col_design_id = col_name.split(".")[0]
        col_design_ids.add(col_design_id)

        for row_name in complex_rmsd_df.index:
            # Extract the design identifier from row name
            # target_name_and_binder_design_id_model_0.cif -> binder_design_id
            # Split on '_and_' and take the second part, then remove model_0.* with regex
            row_parts = row_name.split("_and_")[1]
            row_parts = re.sub(r"_model_\d+.*$", "", row_parts)
            row_design_ids.add(row_parts)

            # Check if the design identifiers match
            if col_design_id == row_parts:
                rmsd_value = complex_rmsd_df.loc[row_name, col_name]
                complex_matching_values.append({"Design": col_name, "RMSD": rmsd_value})
                break  # Found match, move to next column

    # Create and display matching values table
    if complex_matching_values:
        complex_matching_df = pd.DataFrame(complex_matching_values)
        styled_complex_matching = (
            complex_matching_df.style.set_caption(
                "C-alpha RMSD Values (Predicted Complexes vs Input Complexes - Matched by Design ID)"
            )
            .format({"RMSD": "{:.3f}"})
            .background_gradient(cmap="viridis", subset=["RMSD"])
            .hide(axis="index")
        )
        display(styled_complex_matching)

        # Print summary statistics for matching values
        matching_values_array = np.array([v["RMSD"] for v in complex_matching_values])
        finite_matching_values = matching_values_array[~np.isnan(matching_values_array)]

        print(f"\nComplex C-alpha RMSD Summary Statistics (matched values only):")
        print(f"Mean RMSD: {finite_matching_values.mean():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Median RMSD: {np.median(finite_matching_values):.3f} Å")
        print(f"Min RMSD: {finite_matching_values.min():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Max RMSD: {finite_matching_values.max():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Std RMSD: {finite_matching_values.std():.3f} Å")
        print(f"Total matched values: {len(complex_matching_values)}")
        print(f"Finite matched values: {len(finite_matching_values)}")
        print(
            f"NaN matched values: {len(complex_matching_values) - len(finite_matching_values)}"
        )
    else:
        print("No matching design IDs found between columns and rows.")
        print(
            "Column names should end with .pdb and row names should contain '_and' pattern."
        )
        print(col_design_ids)
        print(row_design_ids)


else:
    print(f"Complex RMSD file '{complex_rmsd_file}' not found.")
    print(
        "This analysis requires the RMSD comparison between predicted complexes and input complex structures."
    )
    print(
        "The file should be generated by the RMSD4ALL process in the Nextflow pipeline."
    )
```


## Top 10 High-Scoring Interactions

The table below shows the top 10 interactions from the dataset with the highest ipTM scores.

```{python}
#| label: top-iptm-table
#| message: false

# Sort by ipTM score and get the top 10
top_10_df = df.sort_values('iptm', ascending=False).head(10)

# Select and style the table for better presentation
display_cols = ['id', 'target', 'binder', 'iptm', 'ptm', 'confidence_score']
styled_top_10 = top_10_df[display_cols].style.set_caption("Top 10 High-Scoring Interactions by ipTM") \
                                           .format({'iptm': '{:.3f}', 'ptm': '{:.3f}', 'confidence_score': '{:.3f}'}) \
                                           .background_gradient(cmap='viridis', subset=['iptm']) \
                                           .applymap(color_target_cell, subset=['target']) \
                                           .hide(axis='index')
display(styled_top_10)
```

## Boxplots of ipTM Scores by Target

```{python}
#| label: iptm-boxplots
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
# Order targets by median iptm score for better visualization
target_order = df.groupby('target')['iptm'].median().sort_values(ascending=False).index

# Identify outliers based on 2 standard deviations from the mean for each target
mean_iptm = df.groupby('target')['iptm'].transform('mean')
std_iptm = df.groupby('target')['iptm'].transform('std')
df['is_outlier_iptm'] = (df['iptm'] > mean_iptm + 2 * std_iptm) | (df['iptm'] < mean_iptm - 2 * std_iptm)


sns.boxplot(data=df, x='target', y='iptm', order=target_order, width=0.6, palette=color_map_rgb, showfliers=False)
# Plot inliers and outliers with different styles
sns.stripplot(data=df[~df['is_outlier_iptm']], x='target', y='iptm', order=target_order, 
              size=4, alpha=0.7, jitter=True, color='black')
sns.stripplot(data=df[df['is_outlier_iptm']], x='target', y='iptm', order=target_order,
              size=8, alpha=0.9, jitter=True, color='red', marker='X', edgecolor='black', linewidth=1)

plt.title('ipTM Score Distribution by Target\n(Outliers > 2 std dev shown as red X)', fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel('Target', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('ipTM Score', fontsize=12)
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: iptm-mean-by-target
#| message: false

# Summary statistics, sorted by mean ipTM descending
print("\nipTM Score Summary by Target:")
iptm_summary = df.groupby('target')['iptm'].describe().round(4)
iptm_summary = iptm_summary.sort_values(by='mean', ascending=False)
display(iptm_summary.style.background_gradient(cmap='viridis', subset=['mean', '50%']))
```

## Score Distributions

### Individual Target Histograms
Here we visualize the ipTM score distributions for each target using individual histograms, stacked vertically for easy comparison. The mean and median scores are shown for each target.

```{python}
#| label: individual-histograms
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 12

# Order targets by median ipTM score for better visualization
target_order = df.groupby('target')['iptm'].median().sort_values(ascending=False).index

fig, axes = plt.subplots(
    nrows=len(target_order), 
    ncols=1, 
    figsize=(8, 2 * len(target_order)), 
    sharex=True, 
    sharey=True
)
if len(target_order) == 1:
    axes = [axes]

# Find global min and max for consistent binning across all plots
bins = np.linspace(df['iptm'].min(), df['iptm'].max(), 30)

for i, target in enumerate(target_order):
    ax = axes[i]
    target_scores = df[df['target'] == target]['iptm']
    mean_score = target_scores.mean()
    median_score = target_scores.median()
    
    ax.hist(target_scores, bins=bins, color=color_map_rgb[target], alpha=0.8)
    ax.axvline(mean_score, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label=f'Mean: {mean_score:.2f}')
    ax.axvline(median_score, color='black', linestyle=':', linewidth=2, label=f'Median: {median_score:.2f}')
    
    ax.set_ylabel('Frequency')
    ax.set_title(f'Target: {target}', loc='left', fontsize=10)
    ax.legend()

fig.suptitle('ipTM Score Distribution by Target', fontsize=16)
axes[-1].set_xlabel('ipTM Score') # Set x-label only on the last plot
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.97]) # Adjust for suptitle
plt.show()
```

### Kernel Density Plots
This plot shows the same data using kernel density estimates, which can be useful for visualizing the shape of the distributions.

```{python}
#| label: kde-plots
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.kdeplot(data=df, x='iptm', hue='target', palette=color_map_rgb, fill=True, alpha=0.2)
plt.xlabel('ipTM Score')
plt.ylabel('Density')
plt.title('Kernel Density Estimate of ipTM Scores by Target')
plt.show()

```

## Rank-based Statistics by Target

Here we see the summary statistics for the inverted normalized rank (1 - percentile rank) of each target's ipTM score, calculated within each binder group. A higher value is better.

```{python}
#| label: rank-stats
#| message: false

# Summary statistics by target for inverted normalized rank
rank_stats = df.groupby('target').agg({
    'inv_norm_rank': ['mean', 'median', 'std', 'count']
}).round(4)

rank_stats.columns = ['_'.join(col).strip() for col in rank_stats.columns]
rank_stats = rank_stats.sort_values(by='inv_norm_rank_mean', ascending=False)

# Style the table for better presentation
styled_rank_stats = rank_stats.style.background_gradient(cmap='viridis', subset=['inv_norm_rank_mean']) \
                                   .set_caption("Inverted Normalized Rank Statistics by Target (higher is better)") \
                                   .format('{:.3f}')

display(styled_rank_stats)
```

## Comparing distributions of target ipTM rankings

This analysis performs Wilcoxon rank-sum tests to compare the ipTM score distributions between pairs of targets to determine if there are statistically significant differences between targets.P-values have been adjusted for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg FDR method. Only comparisons with adjusted P-value < 0.05 are listed below.

```{python}
#| label: wilcoxon-tests
#| message: false

from itertools import combinations

targets = df['target'].unique()

# Check if we have enough targets for comparison (need at least 2)
if len(targets) < 2:
    print(f"Only {len(targets)} target(s) found. Need at least 2 targets for statistical comparison.")
    print("Skipping Wilcoxon rank-sum tests.")
else:
    wilcoxon_results = []
    all_comparisons = list(combinations(targets, 2))

    for target1, target2 in all_comparisons:
        scores1 = df[df['target'] == target1]['iptm']
        scores2 = df[df['target'] == target2]['iptm']
        
        # Check if we have enough data points for each target (need at least 1 per target)
        if len(scores1) == 0 or len(scores2) == 0:
            print(f"Warning: No data found for {target1} or {target2}. Skipping comparison.")
            continue
        
        # Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Mann-Whitney U)
        try:
            statistic, p_value = stats.mannwhitneyu(scores1, scores2, alternative='two-sided')
            
            wilcoxon_results.append({
                'Comparison': f"{target1} vs {target2}",
                'Statistic': statistic,
                'P-value': p_value,
            })
        except ValueError as e:
            print(f"Warning: Could not perform test for {target1} vs {target2}: {e}")
            continue

    # Perform multiple testing correction
    results_df = pd.DataFrame(wilcoxon_results)
    if not results_df.empty:
        reject, p_adj, _, _ = multipletests(results_df['P-value'], alpha=0.05, method='fdr_bh')
        results_df['Adjusted P-value'] = p_adj
        results_df['Significant'] = reject

        significant_results = results_df[results_df['Significant']].sort_values(by='Adjusted P-value')
        
        if not significant_results.empty:
            styled_wilcoxon = significant_results[['Comparison', 'Statistic', 'Adjusted P-value']].style.format({
                'Statistic': '{:.1f}', 
                'Adjusted P-value': '{:.4g}'
            }).set_caption("Significant Pairwise Target Comparisons (FDR Corrected)").hide(axis='index')
            display(styled_wilcoxon)
        else:
            print("No significant differences found between targets after FDR correction (p < 0.05).")
    else:
        print("No valid comparisons could be made.")

    print(f"\nTotal pairwise comparisons performed: {len(all_comparisons)}")

```


## Mixed Effects Model Analysis

We fit a mixed effects model, predicting ipTM score as a function of target, with binder as a random effect. The target with the highest coefficient is more predictive of the ipTM score.

```{python}
#| label: mixed-model
#| message: false

try:
    import statsmodels.api as sm
    from statsmodels.formula.api import mixedlm
    
    # Check if we have enough data for mixed effects model
    # Need at least 2 targets and multiple binders for meaningful analysis
    n_targets = df['target'].nunique()
    n_binders = df['binder'].nunique()
    
    if n_targets < 2:
        print(f"Only {n_targets} target(s) found. Need at least 2 targets for mixed effects model.")
        print("Skipping mixed effects model analysis.")
    elif n_binders < 2:
        print(f"Only {n_binders} binder(s) found. Need at least 2 binders for mixed effects model.")
        print("Skipping mixed effects model analysis.")
    else:
        # Fit mixed effects model with target as fixed effect, binder as random effect
        model = mixedlm("iptm ~ C(target)", df, groups=df["binder"])
        result = model.fit()
        
        print("Mixed Effects Model Results:")
        print("=" * 40)
        print(result.summary())
        
        # Create a plot of the fixed-effects coefficients
        params = result.params
        
        # Identify the baseline target (the one used as intercept, typically the first alphabetically)
        baseline_target = sorted(df['target'].unique())[0]
        
        # Extract coefficients into a DataFrame
        # The coefficients from the model are offsets from the baseline (intercept)
        coef_data = []
        # Add the baseline with a coefficient of 0
        coef_data.append({'target': baseline_target, 'coefficient': 0})
        
        for param_name, value in params.items():
            if param_name.startswith("C(target)"):
                # Extract target name from 'C(target)[T.target_name]'
                target_name = param_name.split("[T.")[1][:-1]
                coef_data.append({'target': target_name, 'coefficient': value})

        coef_df = pd.DataFrame(coef_data).sort_values('coefficient', ascending=False)
        
        # Get colors in the correct order for the sorted targets
        ordered_colors = [color_map_rgb[t] for t in coef_df['target']]
        
        # Plot the coefficients
        fig_height = max(6, len(coef_df) * 0.5)
        plt.figure(figsize=(8, fig_height))
        
        sns.barplot(x='coefficient', y='target', data=coef_df, palette=ordered_colors, hue='target', dodge=False)
        
        plt.title('Mixed Effects Model Coefficients', fontsize=16)
        plt.xlabel(f'Coefficient (Offset from Baseline: {baseline_target})', fontsize=12)
        plt.ylabel('Target', fontsize=12)
        plt.axvline(0, color='k', linestyle='--', linewidth=1)
        plt.legend([],[], frameon=False) # Hide legend created by 'hue'
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

except ImportError:
    print("Mixed effects modelling requires statsmodels package. Skipping this analysis.")
```

## Binary Binding Analysis at Different ipTM Cutoffs

```{python}
#| label: binary-binding
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 12

cutoffs = [0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(cutoffs), 1, figsize=(8, 12), sharex=True)
if len(cutoffs) == 1:
    axes = [axes]

binding_stats = []

for i, cutoff in enumerate(cutoffs):
    df[f'bind_{cutoff}'] = df['iptm'] >= cutoff
    
    # Calculate binding proportions
    bind_props = df.groupby('target')[f'bind_{cutoff}'].agg(['sum', 'count'])
    bind_props['proportion'] = bind_props['sum'] / bind_props['count']
    bind_props['cutoff'] = cutoff
    
    binding_stats.append(bind_props[['proportion', 'cutoff']].reset_index())
    
    # Plot
    ax = axes[i]
    bind_props = bind_props.sort_values(by='proportion', ascending=False)
    # Get colors in the right order for the bars
    bar_colors = [color_map_rgb[target] for target in bind_props.index]
    bind_props['proportion'].plot(kind='bar', ax=ax, color=bar_colors, alpha=0.8)
    ax.set_title(f'Binding Proportion (ipTM ≥ {cutoff})', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Proportion Binding')
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
    ax.set_ylim(0, max(1.0, bind_props['proportion'].max() * 1.1))
    
    # Add value labels on bars
    for j, v in enumerate(bind_props['proportion']):
        ax.text(j, v + 0.01, f'{v:.2f}', ha='center', va='bottom')

plt.xlabel('Target')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Combine all binding statistics into a styled table
all_binding_stats = pd.concat(binding_stats, ignore_index=True)
pivot_table = all_binding_stats.pivot(index='target', columns='cutoff', values='proportion').sort_values(by=0.6, ascending=False)

styled_pivot = pivot_table.style.highlight_max(color='lightgreen', axis=0) \
                               .format('{:.3f}') \
                               .set_caption("Binding Proportions at Different Cutoffs") \
                               .background_gradient(cmap='viridis')
display(styled_pivot)
```

## Hierarchical Clustering Heatmap

We hierarchically cluster the binders and targets using cosine distance and average linkage.
This may show trends of which targets are most similar to each other, and which groups of binders have a preference for certain targets.

```{python}
#| label: clustering-heatmap
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 10

# Create pivot table for heatmap (binders vs targets)
heatmap_data = df.pivot_table(values='iptm', index='binder', columns='target', fill_value=0)

# Check if we have enough data for clustering (need at least 2 binders and 2 targets)
if heatmap_data.shape[0] >= 2 and heatmap_data.shape[1] >= 2:
    g = sns.clustermap(heatmap_data, 
                   cmap='viridis', 
                   method='average', # Using average linkage, compatible with cosine distance
                   metric='cosine', # Using cosine distance
                   figsize=(8, 16),
                   cbar_kws={'label': 'ipTM Score'},
                   xticklabels=True,
                   yticklabels=True)
    g.fig.suptitle('Hierarchical Clustering: Binders vs Targets (Cosine Distance, Average Linkage)', y=1.02)
    plt.show()
    
    print(f"Heatmap dimensions: {heatmap_data.shape[0]} binders × {heatmap_data.shape[1]} targets")
else:
    print(f"Insufficient data for clustering heatmap. Need at least 2 binders and 2 targets.")
    print(f"Current data: {heatmap_data.shape[0]} binders × {heatmap_data.shape[1]} targets")
    
    # Show a simple heatmap without clustering if we have at least some data
    if heatmap_data.shape[0] >= 1 and heatmap_data.shape[1] >= 1:
        plt.figure(figsize=(8, max(4, heatmap_data.shape[0] * 0.3)))
        sns.heatmap(heatmap_data, cmap='viridis', cbar_kws={'label': 'ipTM Score'}, 
                   xticklabels=True, yticklabels=True)
        plt.title('ipTM Scores: Binders vs Targets (No Clustering)')
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()
```

## Binder ipTM Across Targets (Parallel Coordinates)

This plot visualizes the performance of each individual binder across the different targets. Each line represents a single binder, allowing you to trace its ipTM score from one target to another. You can hover over a line to see the binder's name and its score for a specific target. Clicking on a binder's name in the legend on the right will highlight all of its interactions.

```{python}
# | label: parallel-coords-plot-altair
# | message: false

# Check if we have enough data for parallel coordinates plot
if len(target_order) < 2:
    print(
        f"Only {len(target_order)} target(s) found. Need at least 2 targets for parallel coordinates plot."
    )
    print("Skipping parallel coordinates visualization.")
else:
    # Re-using the `target_order` from the boxplot cell
    first_target = target_order[0]

    # Get the initial ipTM score for each binder from the first target
    initial_iptm_df = df[df["target"] == first_target][["binder", "iptm"]].rename(
        columns={"iptm": "initial_iptm"}
    )

    # Merge this back into the main dataframe
    df_merged = pd.merge(df, initial_iptm_df, on="binder", how="left")

    # Create the parallel coordinates plot with Altair
    chart = (
        alt.Chart(df_merged)
        .mark_line()
        .encode(
            x=alt.X("target:N", sort=target_order.tolist(), title="Target"),
            y=alt.Y("iptm:Q", title="ipTM Score"),
            color=alt.Color(
                "initial_iptm:Q",
                scale=alt.Scale(scheme="viridis"),
                legend=alt.Legend(title=f"ipTM on {first_target}"),
            ),
            detail="binder:N",  # Use detail to group lines without affecting color
            tooltip=["binder:N", "target:N", "iptm:Q"],
        )
        .properties(title="Binder Performance Across Targets", width=600)
        .interactive()
    )

    chart
```


# Top Target per Binder

This table lists each binder and its highest-scoring target, sorted by the ipTM score of that top interaction.

```{python}
#| label: top-target-per-binder
#| message: false

# Find the row with the max ipTM score for each binder
top_hits_df = df.loc[df.groupby('binder')['iptm'].idxmax()]

# Select and sort the results
top_hits_table = top_hits_df[['binder', 'target', 'iptm']].sort_values('iptm', ascending=False)

# Style the table
styled_top_hits = top_hits_table.style.set_caption("Top Scoring Target for Each Binder") \
                                     .format({'iptm': '{:.3f}'}) \
                                     .applymap(color_target_cell, subset=['target']) \
                                     .hide(axis='index')
display(styled_top_hits)
```
